content.text: Autoscaling AI workloads does not behave the same as autoscaling stateless web services. A web server can be terminated and replaced with no loss of in-flight state. A GPU worker running an LLM completion cannot: the in-flight completion must either be drained or lost. GPU boot times of 5-12 minutes mean that reactive scaling triggers must fire 15 minutes before the traffic peak arrives, not when it arrives. And the metric that drives scaling decisions — for AI workloads — is not CPU utilisation but token queue depth and time-to-first-token.

Autoscaling policies for AI workloads are the rules, metrics, and tooling that govern how compute resources are added or removed in response to changing inference demand. They cover three concerns: the scaling metrics (what signal drives the decision), the scaling behavior (how fast to add or remove instances, with what stabilisation windows), and the scaling safety mechanisms (how to drain in-flight sessions before terminating a worker, how to maintain a minimum warm pool to absorb burst).

For agentic AI systems, the policy must account for workload characteristics that differ fundamentally from standard web services: variable completion time (a short clarification response takes 2 seconds; a 5,000-token analysis takes 90 seconds), multi-turn session continuity (an agent session may span 20 requests and must not be interrupted mid-conversation), and the GPU warm-up cost (a new GPU worker takes 5-12 minutes to load the model weights into VRAM before it can serve requests).

A complete autoscaling policy for AI workloads has two layers: reactive scaling (responding to current metrics) and proactive pre-scaling (anticipating traffic peaks using the demand forecast). Reactive scaling handles unexpected traffic spikes; proactive scaling handles predictable daily, weekly, and event-driven patterns.

An AI workload without a calibrated autoscaling policy defaults to one of two failure modes: over-provisioning (always running enough capacity for peak, wasting 60-80% of compute budget during off-peak hours) or under-provisioning (running at a fixed size that saturates during peak and degrades latency for all users). Neither is acceptable at production scale.

The specific failure modes of under-provisioned AI workloads are qualitatively different from under-provisioned web services. When a web server queue fills, the server returns HTTP 503 and the client retries. When a GPU worker queue fills, the LLM provider throttles and returns 429 errors, but the LLM completion that was in progress is not necessarily completed — the user receives a partial response or no response at all. For agentic AI, this means the agent's chain of tool calls may be interrupted mid-execution, leaving side effects in an inconsistent state.

Proactive scaling prevents this failure mode for predictable peaks: the model knows that Tuesday mornings see 3x baseline traffic, so the autoscaler adds capacity 15 minutes before Tuesday morning begins. Reactive scaling handles the unpredictable residual. The combination of both layers reduces latency SLO violations by 80-90% compared to purely reactive scaling, based on typical diurnal traffic patterns for enterprise AI systems.
Dynamic scaling eliminates the 60-80% compute waste of static over-provisioning during off-peak periods.
Proactive scaling adds capacity before traffic peaks cause queue saturation, maintaining P95 TTFT within the SLO target.
Graceful drain policies ensure in-flight LLM completions and multi-turn agent sessions are never interrupted by a scale-in event.
Proactive pre-scaling converts unpredictable cost spikes into planned, budgeted capacity additions.
A minimum replica floor absorbs moderate traffic bursts without the 8-minute GPU boot latency of cold starts.
GPU boot latency creates a pre-scaling requirement:
Purely reactive scaling cannot respond fast enough; proactive scaling adds complexity in the form of a demand forecast dependency.
Graceful drain of in-flight sessions means scale-in takes 5-10 minutes per pod. Aggressive scale-in policies that do not drain first result in session interruptions.
Maintaining a warm pool of 2+ replicas 24x7 is necessary for burst absorption but represents a fixed cost floor even during zero-traffic periods.
Composite scaling metrics (token queue + TTFT + session count) are more accurate than CPU% but require a custom metrics adapter and introduce operational complexity.
If multi-turn sessions require affinity to a specific worker (for in-memory conversation state), that worker cannot be terminated until the session ends, limiting scale-in flexibility.

The autoscaling system operates through a control loop running every 15 seconds. A custom metrics adapter collects four signals: pending token queue depth (tokens waiting for GPU assignment), P95 time-to-first-token over the last 2 minutes, active multi-turn session count, and GPU memory utilisation. These are combined into a composite score between 0 and 1, where 0 is idle and 1 is fully saturated.

The Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler evaluates the composite score against two thresholds: a scale-out threshold of 0.7 (add replicas when the score exceeds 0.7) and a scale-in threshold of 0.3 (remove replicas when the score drops below 0.3). A stabilisation window of 60 seconds for scale-out (to avoid reacting to transient spikes) and 1,800 seconds for scale-in (to avoid premature scale-in during natural lulls in a peak) prevents oscillation.

Proactive pre-scaling runs as a Kubernetes CronJob every 15 minutes. It reads the 4-hour-ahead peak forecast from the demand forecasting service, computes the required replica count for that peak, and sets the HPA minimum replica floor to that value. This ensures the fleet is sized correctly before the peak begins, without waiting for the reactive control loop to respond.

attrs.alt: Autoscaling architecture: composite metric collection driving proactive pre-scaling and reactive HPA, feeding a GPU worker pool with graceful drain

content.text: Metric sources (queue depth, token throughput, TTFT, active sessions) feed the composite score adapter. The scaler decision engine drives three scaling paths: proactive pre-scaling (forecast-driven), reactive HPA (metrics-driven), and emergency burst scale (immediate). The GPU worker pool enforces graceful drain before scale-in.

This autoscaling architecture is appropriate for AI inference workloads with variable completion time, multi-turn sessions, or diurnal traffic patterns. It is most valuable when GPU boot latency exceeds 5 minutes (making reactive-only scaling insufficient) and when in-flight session interruption is unacceptable for the user experience.

For batch inference workloads (offline processing, no real-time latency SLO), simpler scaling based on queue depth alone is sufficient — session continuity and TTFT are not concerns for batch. For streaming inference with stateless requests (no multi-turn sessions, homogeneous request size), standard CPU-utilisation-based HPA may be adequate if GPU utilisation correlates well with CPU on the worker node.

The complexity of the composite metric adapter and proactive prescaling is not justified for systems with fewer than 50 concurrent users or with static traffic patterns that can be addressed by a fixed minimum replica count. Calibrate the investment in autoscaling sophistication to the operational scale and cost impact.
Step 1: Build the composite metrics adapter

# k8s/autoscaler/composite-metric-adapter.py
"""
Custom metrics adapter: computes composite autoscaling score
from GenAI-specific metrics and exposes it to Kubernetes HPA.
"""
import time
from prometheus_client import start_http_server, Gauge
import requests

# Weights for the composite score
ALPHA = 0.4   # GPU queue depth (normalised)
BETA  = 0.4   # P95 time-to-first-token (normalised)
GAMMA = 0.2   # active multi-turn session depth

QUEUE_DEPTH_MAX   = 10000   # tokens — normalise to 0-1
TTFT_SLO_SECONDS  = 2.0     # P95 TTFT SLO
SESSION_DEPTH_MAX = 500     # active sessions — normalise

composite_score_gauge = Gauge(
    "genai_autoscale_composite_score",
    "Composite autoscaling signal (0=idle, 1=saturated)"
)

def fetch_metrics():
    prom = "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query"
    def q(expr):
        r = requests.get(prom, params={"query": expr}, timeout=5)
        r.raise_for_status()
        result = r.json()["data"]["result"]
        return float(result[0]["value"][1]) if result else 0.0

    queue_depth  = q("sum(genai_pending_tokens_total)")
    ttft_p95     = q("histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(genai_ttft_seconds_bucket[2m])) by (le))")
    active_sessions = q("sum(genai_active_sessions_total)")
    return queue_depth, ttft_p95, active_sessions

def compute_score(queue_depth, ttft_p95, active_sessions) -> float:
    norm_queue   = min(queue_depth / QUEUE_DEPTH_MAX, 1.0)
    norm_ttft    = min(ttft_p95 / TTFT_SLO_SECONDS, 1.0)
    norm_sessions = min(active_sessions / SESSION_DEPTH_MAX, 1.0)
    return ALPHA * norm_queue + BETA * norm_ttft + GAMMA * norm_sessions

if __name__ == "__main__":
    start_http_server(8080)
    while True:
        try:
            queue, ttft, sessions = fetch_metrics()
            score = compute_score(queue, ttft, sessions)
            composite_score_gauge.set(score)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Metric collection error: {e}")
        time.sleep(15)

# k8s/autoscaler/hpa.yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: genai-worker-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: genai-worker
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    - type: Pods
      pods:
        metric:
          name: genai_autoscale_composite_score
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: "0.7"   # scale out when score > 0.7
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60   # react within 1 minute
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 3
          periodSeconds: 60            # add up to 3 pods per minute
        - type: Percent
          value: 50
          periodSeconds: 60            # or 50% of current count
      selectPolicy: Max                # use whichever adds more capacity
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 1800  # wait 30 min before scaling in
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 1
          periodSeconds: 300           # remove at most 1 pod per 5 min
Step 3: Add graceful drain preStop hook

# k8s/autoscaler/preStop-drain.py
"""
PreStop lifecycle hook: drain in-flight sessions before pod termination.
Mounted as a script in the genai-worker container.
"""
import os, time, requests, signal

HEALTH_PORT = int(os.getenv("HEALTH_PORT", "8080"))
MAX_DRAIN_SECONDS = int(os.getenv("MAX_DRAIN_SECONDS", "300"))  # 5 min max drain

def main():
    # Signal worker to stop accepting new requests
    requests.post(f"http://localhost:{HEALTH_PORT}/drain", timeout=5)
    print("Worker set to DRAINING: rejecting new requests")

    # Wait for in-flight sessions to complete
    start = time.time()
    while time.time() - start < MAX_DRAIN_SECONDS:
        resp = requests.get(f"http://localhost:{HEALTH_PORT}/active-sessions", timeout=5)
        count = resp.json()["active_sessions"]
        if count == 0:
            print("All sessions drained. Safe to terminate.")
            return
        print(f"Waiting for {count} active sessions to complete...")
        time.sleep(10)

    print(f"Drain timeout after {MAX_DRAIN_SECONDS}s. Terminating with {count} sessions.")
    # Kubernetes will SIGKILL after terminationGracePeriodSeconds

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# autoscaler/proactive_scaler.py
"""
Proactive pre-scaling: increases minimum replicas before predicted peaks.
Runs as a CronJob every 15 minutes; reads 4-hour-ahead forecast.
"""
from kubernetes import client, config
import requests, datetime

NAMESPACE = "production"
DEPLOYMENT = "genai-worker"
HPA_NAME = "genai-worker-hpa"
FORECAST_API = "http://forecast-service/api/v1/next-4h-peak"
LEAD_MINUTES = 15

def get_4h_peak_forecast() -> float:
    resp = requests.get(FORECAST_API, timeout=10)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["peak_compute_units"]

def compute_units_to_replicas(compute_units: float) -> int:
    # Each replica handles ~500 compute units at 65% utilisation
    return max(2, min(20, int(compute_units / 500 / 0.65) + 1))

def set_hpa_min_replicas(target_min: int):
    config.load_incluster_config()
    hpa_api = client.AutoscalingV2Api()
    hpa = hpa_api.read_namespaced_horizontal_pod_autoscaler(HPA_NAME, NAMESPACE)
    hpa.spec.min_replicas = target_min
    hpa_api.patch_namespaced_horizontal_pod_autoscaler(HPA_NAME, NAMESPACE, hpa)
    print(f"Set HPA minReplicas={target_min} for predicted peak")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    peak = get_4h_peak_forecast()
    needed = compute_units_to_replicas(peak)
    set_hpa_min_replicas(needed)

Minimal policy for a single-model deployment with homogeneous request sizes: CPU-based HPA with a scale-out threshold at 70% CPU, scale-in stabilisation window of 600 seconds, minimum 2 replicas, maximum 10 replicas. The preStop hook sends a drain signal and waits up to 120 seconds for in-flight requests to complete. This is appropriate when GPU boot time is under 3 minutes and request completion time is under 30 seconds.

Full composite-metric policy for a multi-turn agentic AI platform: custom metrics adapter collecting token queue depth, P95 TTFT, and active session count. HPA configured with a composite score threshold of 0.7. Scale-in stabilisation window of 1,800 seconds. PreStop drain timeout of 300 seconds. Proactive CronJob running every 15 minutes to set HPA minimum replicas based on 4-hour-ahead forecast. Business-hours warm-up CronJob adds 3 replicas at 8:45 AM and removes them at 6:15 PM.

A customer-service AI platform experienced daily latency spikes at 9:00 AM when business users logged in after overnight downtime. The reactive HPA would not respond until the composite score exceeded 0.7, which took 4-6 minutes into the spike. GPU boot times of 8 minutes meant new capacity was not available until 12-14 minutes into the spike — by which time P95 TTFT had reached 8 seconds against a 2-second SLO.

The fix: a business-hours CronJob that set the HPA minimum to 6 replicas at 8:30 AM (90 seconds before the typical spike onset, accounting for the 8-minute boot time of 6 replicas added at 8:22 AM). The spike absorption was handled entirely by the pre-provisioned capacity. P95 TTFT during the 9 AM peak dropped from 8 seconds to 1.4 seconds. Monthly GPU cost increased by 12% due to the earlier warm-up, but the SLO violation cost (customer satisfaction impact and support ticket volume) was reduced by 90%.
Using CPU% as the scaling metric:
GPU inference workloads can have low CPU utilisation while the GPU is fully saturated and requests are queuing. CPU% is a poor autoscaling signal for GPU workloads.
Terminating a GPU worker without draining in-flight sessions produces incomplete LLM outputs and broken agent tool-call chains. Always implement a preStop drain hook.

A 60-second stabilisation window for scale-in causes the scaler to remove capacity during natural lulls in a peak (e.g., the 5-minute drop between two traffic bursts), then immediately scale back out when traffic resumes. Use 15-30 minute scale-in windows.
Scaling to zero during off-peak periods saves cost but means the first request after a quiescent period waits 8+ minutes for GPU boot. Maintain a minimum of 2 warm replicas.
If agent sessions require affinity to a specific replica, the scaler must not terminate that replica until the session completes. Implement session-aware drain logic.
Build a composite metric from queue depth + TTFT:
No single metric captures GPU saturation accurately for mixed workloads. The composite score is more robust than any individual metric.
React to sustained load, not transient spikes. A single high score sample should not trigger a scale-out; 4 consecutive 15-second evaluations above threshold should.

Avoid premature scale-in that immediately triggers scale-out when traffic resumes. The cost of maintaining 2 extra replicas for 30 minutes is small compared to the latency impact of a premature scale-in.
Implement preStop drain with a realistic timeout:
The timeout should be 1.5x the P99 completion time for the longest request type. If the longest agent task takes 120 seconds, set the drain timeout to 180 seconds.
Test the scaling policy with load testing before production:

Run a load test that simulates the expected peak pattern. Verify that the composite score reaches the threshold, that new pods boot and become ready before queue depth causes SLO violations, and that scale-in drains correctly.

The custom metrics adapter adds one component to the observability stack. It runs as a Deployment with 2 replicas (for availability) and polls Prometheus every 15 seconds. CPU and memory usage are minimal — the adapter does arithmetic on values fetched from Prometheus, not computation-intensive analysis.

The HPA evaluates the composite metric every 15 seconds (Kubernetes default). For very large fleets (50+ replicas), the HPA's scaling calculations may take a few seconds but this is not a bottleneck in practice. The proactive prescaling CronJob runs in under 1 second per execution.

At high scale (20+ GPU workers), the primary performance concern is cluster autoscaler latency — the time for the underlying node to be provisioned when the HPA requests a new pod that requires a new GPU node. This is entirely determined by the cloud provider and node pool configuration. Use node pool warm pools (pre-provisioned but idle GPU nodes) to reduce node provisioning latency from 5-10 minutes to under 1 minute.
Alternative to HPA for queue-based triggers
Faster node provisioning than Cluster Autoscaler
Dashboard and alert on composite score

The autoscaling system must be reliable: a metrics adapter failure that causes the composite score to return 0 should not trigger a scale-in event. The HPA should be configured with a fallback behaviour: if the custom metric is unavailable, maintain the current replica count rather than scaling to minimum.
Run the composite metrics adapter with 2 replicas and a liveness probe. Ensure Prometheus is available before the adapter starts.
Configure the HPA to treat a metric collection failure as a non-event rather than a scale-in trigger. This prevents the scaler from reducing capacity during observability outages.
Set a hard maximum replica count that cannot be exceeded, preventing runaway cost from a bug in the composite score calculation that produces artificially high values.

Log every scale-out and scale-in event with the composite score value, the triggering metric values, and the resulting replica count. Review scaling events weekly to validate that the policy is behaving as designed.

Think of the autoscaling policy as a thermostat system for a building that takes 12 minutes to heat up. A thermostat that only responds when the temperature drops below threshold will always be late — the building is cold while the heater is warming up. The solution is a predictive thermostat that starts heating before the temperature drops, based on the time of day and occupancy schedule.

The composite score is the thermometer; the HPA is the reactive thermostat; the proactive prescaling CronJob is the schedule-based pre-heating. All three are needed: the thermostat handles unexpected cold snaps, the schedule handles the predictable morning heating requirement, and the thermometer reads the actual temperature so the system knows when it has reached the target.
AI autoscaling metrics must be token-queue-depth and P95 TTFT, not CPU utilisation — GPU inference has low CPU utilisation even when fully saturated.
GPU boot latency of 5-12 minutes makes purely reactive scaling insufficient; proactive pre-scaling using demand forecasts is required for predictable peaks.
Scale-in events must drain in-flight LLM completions and multi-turn agent sessions before terminating a worker; implement a preStop drain hook with a realistic timeout.
Maintain a minimum warm replica pool (2+ replicas) to absorb moderate bursts without cold-start latency.
Set scale-in stabilisation windows of 15-30 minutes to prevent premature scale-in during natural lulls in a peak.
The composite metric adapter must be highly available and the HPA must be configured to maintain current replica count (not scale to minimum) on metric collection failure.
Load-test the scaling policy before production to verify that new capacity boots and becomes ready before queue saturation causes SLO violations.
